contextBody: SkyLine Air's platform team needs a single document that defines what "good" looks like for every Deployment, Service, and PVC. Finance partners edit cost-center lists weekly. SREs add comments explaining namespace exceptions. The file must be human-readable and machine-parseable by the FinOps agent you will build later.

artifact.caption: From examples/tagging-rules.yaml
artifact.content:
# Required tags that must be present on all resources
required_tags:
  - owner
  - environment
  - cost-center
  - application
  - tier

# Airline booking context cost centers
cost_centers:
  - booking-engine      # Core booking logic
  - payment             # Payment processing
  - inventory           # Flight/hotel inventory

question: Why did this course choose YAML over JSON for the tagging policy file?
options: YAML is faster for the Kubernetes API server to parse
YAML supports inline comments and matches how K8 manifests are written
JSON cannot represent nested keys like required_tags
YAML automatically enforces tags on every resource at apply time
hints: Think about who edits this file every week — platform engineers and finance partners, not just machines.
Eliminate options C and D: JSON handles nesting fine, and no file format auto-enforces tags on apply.
Look at the inline comments next to cost-center values — JSON does not support those.
distractorNotes: The API server parses manifests the same way regardless of whether policy is stored as YAML or JSON.
JSON supports nested objects; the issue is editability and comments, not structure.
Tag enforcement requires admission controllers or agents — the file format does not enforce anything.
explanation: YAML supports inline comments (like noting that booking-engine owns core reservation logic) and matches the format of Kubernetes manifests. FinOps policy evolves weekly — teams rename cost centers and add exceptions. JSON's strict syntax and lack of comments make it a poor fit for a living policy document humans edit by hand.
keyTakeaway: Policy files humans edit should match manifest format — YAML with comments, not strict JSON.
learningObjective: Explain why YAML is the right format for FinOps tagging policy in this course
discussionPrompt: Ask the room: where does your org store tagging policy today — wiki, spreadsheet, or code?

contextBody: The FinOps agent will scan every Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, and PVC against tagging-rules.yaml. It checks both canonical tag names and Kubernetes label aliases via label_mappings. A resource can satisfy "application" through app.kubernetes.io/name or app.

artifact.caption: Required tags and label aliases
artifact.content:
required_tags:
  - owner
  - environment
  - cost-center
  - application
  - tier

label_mappings:
  owner:
    - app.kubernetes.io/owner
    - owner
  cost-center:
    - app.kubernetes.io/cost-center
    - cost-center
    - costcenter

question: A Deployment has cost-center and environment labels but is missing owner. Which problem does that create first?
options: The cloud provider refuses to bill untagged resources
Nobody can be held accountable when cost spikes or incidents occur
Kubernetes will evict pods missing the owner label
The deployment cannot receive traffic without an owner tag
hints: Cost-center tells you where to charge spend — but who do you page at 2 AM?
Eliminate C and D: Kubernetes does not evict or block traffic based on FinOps labels.
Owner is the accountability tag — without it, escalation paths break even if cost-center is set.
distractorNotes: Cloud providers bill all resources; missing tags create attribution gaps, not billing refusal.
FinOps labels are organizational metadata — they do not affect kubelet scheduling or eviction.
Services route traffic based on selectors and endpoints, not FinOps ownership labels.
explanation: Without an owner tag, you know where to charge cost (cost-center) but not who owns the workload when something goes wrong. FinOps is about accountability — owner connects spend to a team that can act on it.
keyTakeaway: cost-center attributes spend; owner attributes accountability — both are required.

tryItNext.label: Walk through label_mappings in the policy file

learningObjective: Name the five required tags and explain why owner matters for escalation
discussionPrompt: Which required tag does your team forget most often — owner, cost-center, or tier?

contextBody: The CFO asks which team caused a $45K spike. You pull two Deployments from the SkyLine Air cluster. One is the FinOps baseline; the other has partial tags — enough to look compliant at a glance but missing critical ownership metadata.

artifact.caption: booking-api vs inventory-service labels
artifact.content:
# booking-api (namespace: booking-api)
labels:
  app.kubernetes.io/name: booking-api
  app.kubernetes.io/tier: backend
  cost-center: booking-engine
  environment: prod
  # Missing: owner

---

# inventory-service (namespace: inventory)
labels:
  app.kubernetes.io/name: inventory-service
  cost-center: inventory
  environment: prod
  owner: inventory-team
  criticality: P2

question: Which workload should the FinOps agent flag as an ownership gap?
options: inventory-service — it has too many labels, creating noise
booking-api — it has cost-center but no owner label
Both equally — any missing tag is equally severe
Neither — cost-center alone is sufficient for FinOps compliance
hints: Compare the two label blocks side by side — one has owner, one does not.
inventory-service is the green baseline in this course — look for the deployment that is incomplete.
booking-api has cost-center and environment but the comment says # Missing: owner.
distractorNotes: More labels is not a FinOps problem — inventory-service is the properly tagged baseline.
Missing owner blocks accountability immediately; other missing tags matter but owner is the gap here.
cost-center alone cannot tell you who to page or who approved the spend.
explanation: booking-api has partial compliance — cost-center and environment are set, but owner is missing. That creates a billing blind spot: you can allocate cost to booking-engine but cannot escalate to a team. inventory-service is the properly tagged baseline in Section 03.
keyTakeaway: Partial tagging is worse than no tagging at scale — it looks compliant until an incident or spike hits.

tryItNext.label: Inspect booking-api in the cluster
tryItNext.command: kubectl get deployment booking-api -n booking-api -o yaml

learningObjective: Identify a workload missing required FinOps tags against the SkyLine Air baseline
discussionPrompt: Ask the room: have you ever had a cost spike where nobody claimed ownership?

contextBody: Scanning PVCs across the cluster, you find a 5Gi claim in the airline namespace. It has a minimal app label but no pod mounts it. Storage keeps billing every hour whether a workload uses it or not.

artifact.caption: problem-resources/orphaned-pvc.yaml
artifact.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: orphaned-data-pvc
  namespace: airline
  labels:
    app: old-service   # minimal — no owner or cost-center
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi

question: Why is this PVC FinOps debt even though it has an app label?
options: The app label proves it belongs to a retired service — no action needed
It burns storage cost with no running workload and lacks ownership tags for escalation
PVCs in the airline namespace are excluded from cost allocation
Orphaned PVCs are automatically deleted after 30 days by Kubernetes
hints: A label alone does not mean a pod is using the storage — check whether anything mounts this claim.
Eliminate C and D: airline is not excluded, and Kubernetes does not auto-delete orphaned PVCs.
No owner or cost-center means nobody will claim this 5Gi/month spend in a finance review.
distractorNotes: A generic app label does not retire the claim or stop billing — something must delete or reclaim it.
Only system namespaces like kube-system are excluded in tagging-rules.yaml, not airline.
Orphaned PVCs persist indefinitely unless someone deletes them — there is no automatic cleanup.
explanation: This PVC is orphaned — no pod mounts it, so the 5Gi allocation burns cost idle. The minimal app: old-service label does not satisfy required tags (owner, cost-center) and gives no escalation path. This is exactly the kind of resource a FinOps agent should flag for tech-debt review.
keyTakeaway: Orphaned PVCs are silent cost leaks — label completeness and mount status both matter.
learningObjective: Recognize an orphaned PVC as idle storage burn and untracked FinOps debt
discussionPrompt: Ask the room: who owns storage cleanup in your org — platform, finance, or individual teams?

contextBody: A ConfigMap sits in the default namespace with zero labels. It is not part of any team's namespace isolation strategy. When the agent scans for ownership, this object has no team, no cost-center, and no application identity.

artifact.caption: problem-resources/untracked-configmap.yaml
artifact.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: untracked-config
  namespace: default
  # No labels — will be flagged as unknown
data:
  key: "value"

question: What is the primary FinOps risk of this ConfigMap?
options: ConfigMaps in default cannot be read by application pods
It is invisible to cost and ownership attribution — a ghost resource
default namespace resources are free and therefore ignored by finance
ConfigMaps do not need labels because they carry no cloud cost
hints: Think about what happens when this ConfigMap is connected to a bill line item — can you name the owner?
Eliminate A and C: default namespace objects work fine; nothing in K8 is "free."
Zero labels + default namespace = no team, no cost-center, no escalation path.
distractorNotes: Pods in any namespace can reference ConfigMaps if RBAC allows — namespace alone is not the blocker.
All resources should be attributable; default namespace often means "nobody claimed this."
ConfigMaps enable workloads that do incur compute and operational cost — they need ownership too.
explanation: An unlabeled ConfigMap in default is a ghost resource — it appears in cluster scans but cannot be mapped to a team or cost-center. At scale, hundreds of these make grep-based audits fail and block accurate chargeback.
keyTakeaway: Untracked ConfigMaps are governance gaps — not costly alone, but they signal broken ownership hygiene.
learningObjective: Explain why unlabeled resources in default namespace create ownership blind spots
discussionPrompt: How many unlabeled objects do you think hide in default namespace in a typical prod cluster?

contextBody: SkyLine Air runs across 12 team namespaces plus problem-resources in default and airline. The CFO wants a weekly ownership report. Your teammate proposes running manual kubectl get ... --show-labels | grep across every resource type before each finance meeting.

artifact.caption: Manual audit approach (does not scale)
artifact.content:
$ kubectl get deployments -A --show-labels | grep -v owner
$ kubectl get services -A --show-labels | grep -v owner
$ kubectl get pvc -A --show-labels
$ kubectl get configmaps -A --show-labels
# ... repeat for every resource type, every week

question: What is the best next step for sustainable FinOps governance at SkyLine Air?
options: Mandate separate cloud accounts per team to avoid tagging
Build an agent that reads tagging-rules.yaml and scans the cluster automatically
Assign one FinOps analyst to run grep weekly and email spreadsheets
Disable all namespaces except kube-system until tags are fixed
hints: The course goal is an agent pipeline: Collect → Analyze → Act. Which option matches that?
Eliminate A and D: separate accounts and disabling namespaces kill velocity without fixing root cause.
Manual grep works for a demo cluster of six services — not for hundreds of services in production.
distractorNotes: Separate accounts add overhead and do not solve shared resources or label inconsistency within a cluster.
Manual grep does not scale — it is the pain point this entire course section sets up.
Disabling namespaces is not a governance strategy — it stops the business from running.
explanation: Manual grep fails at production scale — resource types multiply, labels drift weekly, and spreadsheets go stale. The course path is an agent that reads policy-as-YAML and scans automatically, producing structured findings (and later, tech-debt issues). That is Inform → Optimize → Operate at cluster scale.
keyTakeaway: Policy-as-code + automated scanning beats manual kubectl grep for ongoing FinOps governance.
learningObjective: Justify automated policy scanning over manual grep for cluster-scale FinOps
discussionPrompt: What would break first if you relied on weekly manual grep — speed, accuracy, or morale?
